popup: Leonard Road
Heathdene Road

Wallis's Cottages

Likely named after James Wallis, who owned a number of cottages in the mid 19th century (Gower, 2004, pp. 16)

Upper Tulse Hill

Named after Sir Henry Tulse, who occupied the land during the Commonwealth. (Sheppard, 1956)
Wellington Mews
Sherwood Avenue
Barker Walk

Lewin Road

Named after the Lewin Family, but noteably Benjamin Lewin, 'A man greatly respected by all who had the pleasure of his aquaintance' as his gravestone in St. Leonard's Churchyard described. (Brown, 2001, pp. 11)
Penrith Street

Namba Roy Close

Namba Roy was a Jamaican novelist and artist, who settled in Streathm Hill.

Campbell Close

This can also be found on the female map as it is a unisex name.

James Boswell Close

A Scottish biographer and diarist.

St Andrews Mews

Named after St. Andrew the Apostle

Fisher's Close

Potentially named after Samuel Fisher, son-in-law of Beriah Drew, as himself, his wife, his other son-in-law Charles Mortimer, and his wife, Jane Angles Fisher have streets named after them. However this is not to be taken as fact.

Eardley Road

This road, once a trackway across open farmland (Bloice, 2000, pp. 15), was named in honour of the first vicar of Immanuel Church: Reverend Stenton Eardley, who served the parish from 1854-83 (Brown, 2001, pp. 8).

St Leonard's Walk

Saint Leonard of Noblac was a Frankish nobleman.

Potters Lane

Named after a local farmer, James Potter, 1771-1836 (Brown, 2001, pp. 10).

Blegborough Road

Named in honour of Dr. Ralph Blegborough, who purchased Furzedown Farm in 1824 (Brown, 2001, pp. 10).
Allen Close
Mulberry Close
Milford Mews
Hartwell Close
Wyatt Park Road
Leigham Vale
Goodman Crescent
Leigham Avenue
Prescott Close

Samuel Johnson Close

Named after Samuel Johnson, an English writer and polymath. 'There is nothing which has yet been contrived by man by which so much happiness is provided as by a good tavern or inn' - Samuel Johnson, 21 March 1776 on The Leigham Arms Pub (Brown, 2003, pp. 11)

Thornton Avenue

Named after Henry Thornton, the Clapham philanthropist (Gower, 2004, pp.6).
Vibart Gardens

William Dyce Mews

Named after William Dyce, a British painter and a Churchwarden of St. Leonard (1862-1864). he was buryied in St. Leonard's Churchyard. (Brown, 2003, pp.6)
Baxter Walk
Brunswick Mews

Drewstead Road

Supposedly named by and after Beriah Drew, Lord of the Manor of Leigham.
Leigham Close

Russell's Footpath

Named in 1936.

Ellison Road

This can also be found on the female map as it is a unisex name.
Colwyn Close
Beckett Close

De Montfort Road

Likely named after Simon de Montfort, 6th Earl of Leicester.
Emmanuel Road
Eastwood Street

Thomas Hardy Mews

An English novelist and poet

Mortimer Close

Supposedly named after Charles Mortimer, son-in-law of Beriah Drew, Lord of the Manor of Leigham.
Colson Way

Rama Close

Rama is a popular Hindu diety, and he is known to be an avatar of the Lord Vishnu.

Byron Close

Originally named Sussex Gardens, but was changed and named after Lord Byron (Brown, 2001, pp. 12)
Albert Carr Gardens

Masters Close

While not necessarily a male name, the word 'Master' has heavy male connotations in which I though should be included on this map.
St Julian's Farm Road
Nimrod Road
Garrad's Road

Bedford Hill

Likely named after the Duke of Bedford, who had Bedford House named after him (Brown, 2003, pp.8).
Leigham Court Road
Montrell Road

Francis Barber Close

Francis Barber was the Jamaican manservant and companion of Dr. Samuel Johnson (The Black People of Streatham, The Streatham Society)

Henry Tate Mews

Sir Henry Tate, a British sugar Merchant and sugar baker, bought a house on the street.

Curtis Field Road

Likely named after John Curtis
Conyer's Road
Romeyn Road
Hearn Place
Dorrien Walk

Telford Avenue

This Road was named after Chales Telford, a city stockbroker, in 1881, even though it was his wife, Sarah Kymer's, land that the Telford Park Estate was built on (Gower, 2004, pp.6)

Westcote Road

This road was orinially called Thrale Road, but renamed in honour of Lord Westcote (Bloice, 2000, pp.15)

Kingsmead Road

While not necessarily a male name, the word 'King' has heavy male connotations in which I though should be included on this map.

Kingscourt Road

While not necessarily a male name, the word 'King' has heavy male connotations in which I though should be included on this map.
